abstract: Measuring visual perception is a foundational yet challenging problem in computer vision. While mathematical models effectively quantify technical degradations like noise and blur, they remain loosely coupled with natural language and struggle to capture the more abstract "feel" of an image. Assessing such subjective qualities typically requires supervised models trained on laborious, human-labeled datasets. In this paper, we move beyond conventional paradigms by leveraging the rich visual-language priors of Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) to assess both image quality (look) and abstract perception (feel) without task-specific training. We introduce an antonym prompt pairing strategy that effectively harnesses CLIP’s latent knowledge while mitigating linguistic ambiguity. Extensive experiments across controlled datasets and standard Image Quality Assessment (IQA) benchmarks demonstrate that CLIP-IQA captures meaningful perceptual priors that generalize across diverse aesthetic and technical dimensions.
